title: SQL
description: Database queries. SELECT, JOIN, subqueries, indexes, transactions, normalization, and performance tuning.

items.label: Basic SELECT
WHERE & Filtering
items.code:
-- Comparison operators
SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 18;
SELECT * FROM products WHERE price BETWEEN 10 AND 50;
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE 'J%';      -- starts with J
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name LIKE '%son%';    -- contains 'son'
SELECT * FROM users WHERE email IS NOT NULL;

-- IN clause
SELECT * FROM users WHERE country IN ('USA', 'UK', 'Canada');

-- Multiple conditions
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'active'
  AND total > 100
  AND (created_at >= '2025-01-01' OR priority = 'high');

-- String functions
UPPER(name), LOWER(name), LENGTH(name), TRIM(name)
SUBSTRING(name, 1, 3) -- first 3 chars
REPLACE(name, 'old', 'new')

items.label: GROUP BY & HAVING
items.code:
-- Aggregate functions
SELECT COUNT(*), MAX(price), MIN(price), AVG(price), SUM(quantity)
FROM products;

-- Group by
SELECT country, COUNT(*) AS user_count
FROM users
GROUP BY country;

-- Group by with HAVING (filter after group)
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;

-- Multiple levels
SELECT state, city, COUNT(*) AS count
FROM users
GROUP BY state, city
ORDER BY count DESC;

-- Group with ROLLUP
SELECT department, SUM(salary)
FROM employees
GROUP BY ROLLUP(department);

items.label: JOIN Types
items.code:
-- INNER JOIN (only matching rows)
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- LEFT JOIN (all users, even without orders)
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- RIGHT JOIN (all orders, even without user)
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
RIGHT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- FULL OUTER JOIN (all from both)
SELECT u.name, o.total
FROM users u
FULL OUTER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- CROSS JOIN (cartesian product)
SELECT * FROM products CROSS JOIN categories;

-- Self JOIN
SELECT e1.name AS employee, e2.name AS manager
FROM employees e1
JOIN employees e2 ON e1.manager_id = e2.id;

-- Multiple JOINs
SELECT u.name, o.total, p.name AS product
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id
JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.id;

items.label: Subqueries
CTE (WITH clause)
items.code:
-- Common Table Expression
WITH dept_avg AS (
  SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY department
)
SELECT e.name, e.department, e.salary, d.avg_sal
FROM employees e
JOIN dept_avg d ON e.department = d.department
WHERE e.salary > d.avg_sal;

-- Recursive CTE (hierarchy/org tree)
WITH RECURSIVE org_tree AS (
  SELECT id, name, manager_id, 1 AS level
  FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL
  UNION ALL
  SELECT e.id, e.name, e.manager_id, t.level + 1
  FROM employees e
  JOIN org_tree t ON e.manager_id = t.id
)
SELECT * FROM org_tree;

items.label: Data Modification
CREATE & ALTER
Transactions
Window Functions
items.code:
-- ROW_NUMBER
SELECT name, department, salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank
FROM employees;

-- RANK / DENSE_RANK
SELECT name, salary,
  RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rank,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dense_rank
FROM employees;

-- LAG / LEAD (previous/next row)
SELECT date, amount,
  LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_day,
  LEAD(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS next_day
FROM daily_sales;

-- Running total
SELECT date, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS running_total
FROM sales;

-- Moving average
SELECT date, amount,
  AVG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)
    AS moving_avg_7day
FROM sales;

items.label: Query Optimization
items.code:
-- EXPLAIN plan
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'test@test.com';

-- Index usage
CREATE INDEX ON users (email);  -- B-tree default
CREATE INDEX ON users USING GIN (tags);  -- for array/JSON
CREATE INDEX ON products USING GIST (location);  -- for geo

-- Partial index
CREATE INDEX ON users (email) WHERE active = true;

-- Covering index
CREATE INDEX ON users (email) INCLUDE (name, age);

-- Slow query signs
-- Seq Scan on large tables (missing index)
-- Sort with high memory usage (missing index)
-- Nested Loop on large datasets

-- Best practices
-- 1. Use indexes on WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY columns
-- 2. Avoid SELECT * (fetch only needed columns)
-- 3. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to find bottlenecks
-- 4. Partition large tables
-- 5. Use connection pooling
-- 6. Regular VACUUM and ANALYZE
